component_identification.content: Power Grid includes a double-sided map, power plant deck and market, resource market (coal, oil, garbage, uranium), house pieces in player colors, money, and summary cards. Plants have auction cost, resource needs, and city capacity. Map regions have connection costs and city occupancy limits that change by game step.

core_game_loop.content: Each round has phases: determine player order, auction one plant per player, buy resources, build network connections/houses, then bureaucracy (generate power, consume resources, and earn income). Turn order reverses between some phases, creating catch-up pressure: leaders act later in auctions/resources but earlier in building.

detailed_rules.content: Initial setup removes map regions based on player count and seeds plant deck with Step cards. Auction phase is mandatory for at least one eligible player each round; bidding discipline is crucial because overpaying slows expansion. Resource prices escalate as supply shrinks, so timing purchases and fuel flexibility matter. Building phase pays both connection costs and house placement costs; network positioning can block opponents and secure cheap expansion corridors. Bureaucracy income depends on number of cities powered, with diminishing returns table encouraging efficiency. Plant market cycles as lowest/highest plants are removed/replaced by deck draws, and Step 2/3 alter city occupancy limits and market behavior. You can hold up to three plants; replacing old inefficient plants at the right moment is key. Endgame triggers when a player reaches target connected cities, then winner is most cities powered (not merely connected), tiebreaking by money.

scoring_and_endgame.content: When endgame trigger threshold is met, complete round and conduct final bureaucracy. Player who powers the most cities wins; ties break by largest cash reserve, then connected cities. Throughout play, economic tempo and fuel access typically matter more than raw plant count.

beginner_strategy.content: Avoid bidding wars early unless a plant clearly upgrades your efficiency. Buy fuel ahead of demand spikes when cheap, but avoid hoarding mismatched resources. Expand steadily without overbuilding cities you cannot power. Track turn order implications every round; being first or last can radically change auction/resource outcomes. Keep one flexible plant mix so market shocks in a resource type do not stall you.
